dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 at Richmond for suspected cardiac arrest.
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT. Can you tell me what happened today?
She had a seizure, foaming at the mouth, and rolling around.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where you are?
Can you squeeze my hand for me?
Her last seizure was in middle school.
She was jerking and the insulin pump kept beeping.
She's diabetic and uses an insulin pump daily.
No known drug allergies.
Airway appears clear. I'll manage it with positioning to ensure patency. May I adjust her head?
Pulse is elevated at 128, blood pressure is 127 over 51.
We recorded a pulse of 128 beats per minute upon initial assessment.
Respiratory rate measured 59 breaths per minute.
Her blood pressure is 127/51 at this moment.
Blood glucose is 157 mg/dL.
SpO₂ remains at 0% during the first reading.
The EKG currently shows sinus rhythm.
Ma'am, can you tell me what year it is today?
She vomited green outside when she was moved.
Are you currently experiencing any pain?
She reported no pain, just confusion and disorientation.
She is insulin pump dependent and on Lantus.
Is she currently aware of time or people around her?
She regained awareness in the ED, but was A&O x 2 earlier.
After 26 minutes, her pulse is at 123 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate has decreased to 16 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 122/69.
SpO₂ improved to 84 percent in the ambulance.
The EKG remains in sinus rhythm.
Blood glucose is still showing zero at this time.
Respiratory rate is 14 breaths per minute.
Pulse has decreased to 108 beats per minute.
Blood pressure recorded as 109 over 65.
Her SpO₂ is 92 percent now, a clear improvement.
We're placing a four-lead ECG now as per protocol.
We started a 16-gauge IV in the arm successfully.
We administered 4 mg of Zofran for vomiting.
Per the protocol, she's being managed as postictal or status epilepticus.
Based on her presentation, we are transporting her to VCU ED.
She was able to walk to the stretcher under supervision.
She is being kept in a semi-Fowler's position, with belts for safety.
Vitals and interventions have been reassessed intermittently during transport.
She is currently stable and in a semi-upright seated position.
We handed over directly to the ED RN at VCU.
She walked from stretcher to the ED bed independently.
Only one dose of Zofran was administered and she showed improvement.
She was postictal but now alert with a GCS score of 15.
Initial GCS was 14 and improved spontaneously by the time of transport.
Call type was reported as cardiac arrest; however, patient regained stability.
She follows the seizure protocol with no signs of current convulsions or ongoing status.
Continuing to monitor for respiratory changes due to medical history and postictal state.
No bruises, rashes, or bite marks noted on skin inspection.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
No drainage or foreign body found during oral or nasal exam.
Bilateral breath sounds and heart sounds remain clear.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender with normal bowel sounds.
No deformities, wounds, or sensory deficits observed in limbs or spine.
She also had swelling in her mouth, which contributed to the green vomit.
Oxygen was administered via NC during transport to support SpO₂ now at 92%.
The patient was secured to the stretcher using seat belts for safe transport.
Transport site is VCU Health Systems under stretcher placement.
Patient arrived seated and secured onto the ED bed without incident.
Confirmed GCS of 15 upon transferring to the ED and no further interventions needed.
It all began after she had insulin pump beeping while seizing.
EKG remains sinus rhythm with no new changes.
IV was used for hydration and as preparation for any immediate meds if needed.
She was ambulatory to the stretcher and needed minimal assistance.
No last food or drinks were reported by bystanders.
She is able to follow commands and speak now before being placed in the ED bed.
She didn't eat anything today, as she usually eats light to manage sugar.
No abnormalities in urine output or genital inspection.
Breathing appears stable with no signs of retractions or use of accessory muscles.
No lymph nodes were palpable nor was there any neck stiffness.
No signs of jaundice or pallor observed on skin inspection.
Glucose was consistently 0 after initial reading of 157, confirming no hyper/hypoglycemia.
Current assessment aligns with postictal status, no indication of status epilepticus now.
All EKG readings remain in sinus rhythm with no significant deviation noted.
All securement devices were confirmed before leaving the residence.
The decision to transport was made due to medical history of seizure and postictal confusion.
VCU was chosen for direct triage to neuro or ER as needed.
She moved independently to stretcher but required supervision due to confusion.
Once in the ambulance, she was placed in semi-Fowler's for breathing comfort.
No new findings reported in breathing status now after early improvement.
Ultimately, blood pressures remained stable, and no perfusion concerns were noted.
We completed the secondary assessment and found no additional signs of trauma or sickness.
Final recorded respiratory rate was 14 and vitals remained within stable range after transport.
The final BP was 109/65, showing gradual normalization through paramedic care.
Oxygen saturation improved to 92% in the ambulance and no further oxygenation changes noted.
Final heart rate was 108; patient demonstrated gradual cardiovascular recovery.
All data and vitals were relayed to the ED notes and a full EMT report was printed.
No further protocol action was required, as patient was safely transitioned to emergency care.
All necessary transport prep was completed and the transfer to VCU ED was successful.
No additional medications were given after Zofran to manage current condition.
She was ambulatory at baseline but required assistance during movement.
ED staff were briefed on respiratory support, hydration management, and current state.
EMS protocol was followed as per status epilepticus, and no new protocols triaged during assessment.
The final ED was confirmed before departure and location of transport was properly noted.
The patient transitioned directly from stretcher to bed with no modification needed due to good mobility.
EMS care is concluded. The patient is in stable condition and under observation at the ED.
Patient is now in the care of the VCU hospital and no further EMS action is required.
